generated_question: What are the typical root diseases affecting peas and what are the effective management strategies?
generated_answer: Root diseases in peas represent a major challenge in cultivation, leading to significant yield losses and plant mortality. The primary root diseases affecting peas include Root rot (caused by Fusarium oxysporum and Rhizoctonia solani). These soil-borne fungal pathogens attack the root system, impairing the plant's ability to absorb water and nutrients, ultimately resulting in stunted growth, wilting, and plant death. Disease Description and Symptoms: Root diseases in peas typically present through a combination of above-ground and below-ground symptoms. Above-ground symptoms include yellowing of lower leaves (chlorosis), stunted plant growth, wilting during hot periods despite adequate soil moisture, and premature plant death. Below-ground examination reveals the most characteristic symptoms: darkened, water-soaked roots that turn brown to black, root decay and rot, reduced root mass, and absence of healthy white root tips. generated_question: What is the recommended foliar spray composition of NPK 20:20:13 and magnesium Sulphate per acre for maize crop?
generated_answer: There is no fertilizer named 20:20:13. However, farmer must have mistaken it with 20:20:0:13 fertilizer. 20:20:0:13 supplied nitrogen, phosphorus and sulphur. 100kg of this fertilizer is said to supply 20kg Nitrogen, 20kg phosphorus and 13kg sulphur to the field. This fertilizer is not said to supply potassium fertilizer.  NP(S) 20-20-13 should be applied to the soil considering important factors such as placement, proportion and time of crop cycle. It should be applied during sowing and through broadcasting. The dosage should be as per the crop and the soil (As per general recommendation for the State). It is advised not to use the NP(S) 20-20-0-13 with standing crops, application of NP(S) 20-20-0-13 through seed- cum fertilizer gives better outputs. There is no mention of its usage as foliar spray. However, since it is strictly advised to be applied as soil application, farmers are recommended not to use this fertilizer for foliar spray.  Magnesium sulphate fertilizer is suggested @

generated_question: What are the control measures for Bihar hairy caterpillar and gram caterpillar in Berseem crop?
generated_answer: Bihar hairy caterpillar and gram caterpillar are significant pests that can cause substantial damage to Berseem (Egyptian clover) crops, particularly during the vegetative growth stages. Understanding their biology and implementing integrated pest management strategies is crucial for effective control.   1. Bihar hairy caterpillar (Bhabu Kuta): It is polyphagous and sporadic pest. It attacks berseem crop twice in a season. - In September-October larvae devour the young crop to the ground level and the farmers have to resow it. Again, it feeds voraciously on plant foliage and causes severe losses in March-April.  Control: - Weeds like Gutpatna, Bathu, Jangli Palak and wild castor are the alternate host plants on which the pest feeds in gregarious phase and later on migrates to berseem. Destroy these weeds growing adjoining to berseem fields before its sowing.  2. generated_question: What is the proper method for boll opening (glooming) in cotton?
generated_answer: Blooming and boll opening are both different stages of cotton crop. Blooming is flowering of cotton crop while boll opening is opening of the boll or bursting of boll of cotton crop.  Blooming: Blooming is flowering of the cotton crop. It has 2 types or stages of blooming- Early blooming and peak blooming stages. At early bloom, some cotton plants may not yet have started flowering, while others produce blooms at the first position on the first fruiting branch. As the remaining plants begin to flower, blooms progress upward along the main stem and outward along the fruiting branches, leading to an increase in the flowering rate.  The bloom rate rises rapidly and could increase geometrically if square retention were perfect and if there were no competition between or within plants. The rate reaches a maximum depending on the availability of essential resources such as moisture, light, carbohydrates, and mineral nutrients.
